title: HTTP/2 Multiplexing vs HTTP/3 over QUIC
description:
## Why does HTTP/3 still help when HTTP/2 already multiplexes?

An engineer notes that HTTP/2 already multiplexes many requests over a single connection, yet on lossy mobile networks a single dropped packet still stalls all the in-flight streams. They ask why HTTP/3 fixes this. Which statement is correct?

options.text: HTTP/2 multiplexes concurrent streams over one TCP connection and compresses headers (HPACK), but because TCP guarantees in-order byte delivery, one lost segment blocks delivery of all multiplexed streams (transport-level head-of-line blocking). HTTP/3 runs over QUIC, which is built on UDP and implements independent per-stream reliability, so a lost packet only stalls its own stream — eliminating TCP head-of-line blocking; QUIC also integrates the TLS 1.3 handshake to reduce connection setup latency.
HTTP/2 already eliminates head-of-line blocking entirely; HTTP/3's only benefit is mandatory server push, which HTTP/2 lacks.
HTTP/3 runs over TCP with a new framing layer; its advantage is that it disables header compression, which had caused the CRIME attack in HTTP/2.
HTTP/2 uses a separate TCP connection per stream to avoid head-of-line blocking, while HTTP/3 multiplexes them all onto one UDP datagram per response, trading reliability for speed.

explanation: HTTP/2 multiplexes many logical streams over a **single TCP connection** and compresses headers with HPACK. But TCP delivers a single in-order byte stream, so if one segment is lost, the kernel withholds all subsequent bytes — including those belonging to other, fully-received HTTP/2 streams — until the gap is retransmitted. That is **transport-layer head-of-line blocking**, which HTTP/2 does *not* solve (refuting (b)). HTTP/3 runs over **QUIC, a transport on top of UDP**, that maintains independent reliability and ordering per stream, so a packet loss only delays the affected stream and the others proceed. QUIC also folds in the TLS 1.3 cryptographic handshake, cutting connection-establishment round trips. (c) is wrong: HTTP/3 is over UDP/QUIC, not TCP, and it uses QPACK header compression rather than disabling compression. (d) is wrong on both counts: HTTP/2 uses one connection (not one TCP connection per stream), and HTTP/3 does not cram a whole response into a single UDP datagram nor abandon reliability — QUIC provides reliable, ordered per-stream delivery.

hints.title: Two layers of HOL blocking
hints.body: HTTP/1.1 had application-layer HOL blocking; HTTP/2 fixed that with multiplexing but is still subject to TCP's transport-layer HOL blocking on packet loss.

hints.title: QUIC's key move
hints.body: By running over UDP and tracking loss/ordering per stream itself, QUIC ensures one lost packet only stalls its own stream, and it merges transport + TLS setup.
